meta.title: Thinking Like the Machine: a data-engineering concept landscape

edge_legend.contains: instinct hub to its concept node, directional
edge_legend.related: two concepts linked via a Related term, same family, undirected
edge_legend.cross-family: two concepts linked via a Related term, different families, undirected
edge_legend.depends-on: concept draws on another instinct, directional

instincts.title: Physical form dominates performance.
instincts.framing: Logical data types are an abstraction. Every value has a physical byte layout, and that layout, not the logical type, sets the ceiling on scan and compute throughput.

instincts.title: Movement is the cost.
instincts.framing: CPU cycles are cheap relative to moving bytes across copies, processes, disks, and networks. Expert design minimizes and amortizes movement.

instincts.title: Batch beats tuple-at-a-time.
instincts.framing: Per-item overhead from virtual calls, bounds checks, and cache misses dominates when you process one value at a time. Processing batches amortizes that overhead and unlocks SIMD.

instincts.title: Declare what; let the planner choose how.
instincts.framing: You declare intent as SQL or a DataFrame. The engine derives a logical plan, rewrites it, and chooses a physical plan from statistics. Separating what from how is what lets the same query get faster with no rewrite.

instincts.title: The fastest work is skipped work.
instincts.framing: I/O you never issue is free. Layouts and indexes that let the engine prove a chunk is irrelevant beat any amount of faster scanning.

instincts.title: Approximate on purpose.
instincts.framing: Exact answers are often unaffordable and unnecessary. Bounded-error structures give orders-of-magnitude savings for cardinality, quantiles, and similarity.

instincts.title: Placement decides what is cheap.
instincts.framing: In a distributed engine the cost of an operation is set by whether the data it needs is already co-located. Joins and aggregations are cheap when keys are aligned and expensive when they force a shuffle.

instincts.title: Durability is an append-only log plus a snapshot.
instincts.framing: Databases do not overwrite data in place and hope for the best. They append every change to a write-ahead log for durability and keep a snapshot or index for fast reads. Recovery replays the log onto the last snapshot.

instincts.title: Time and ordering are plural and uncertain.
instincts.framing: There is no single notion of when. Event time, ingestion time, and processing time differ, ordering holds only within a partition, and completeness is something you estimate with watermarks rather than know for certain.

instincts.title: Design every pipeline to be safe to re-run.
instincts.framing: Failures, backfills, and replays are normal operations. A pipeline you cannot run twice is a liability. Idempotency, determinism, and explicit history make re-running boring and safe.

instincts.title: A table is metadata over immutable files.
instincts.framing: On object storage there is no in-place update and no atomic rename. A table is a metadata layer that points at a set of immutable files, and a commit swaps metadata rather than data. This is the entire premise of the lakehouse.

instincts.title: Compose interchangeable layers through open standards.
instincts.framing: Modern systems are not monoliths. Storage, catalog, execution engine, and query frontend are separate layers joined by open formats and plan intermediate representations, so each can be swapped independently.

instincts.title: A system that cannot say no will fail.
instincts.framing: Unbounded acceptance leads to collapse. Memory budgets, backpressure, admission control, and scheduling let a system slow or shed work and degrade predictably instead of crashing.

instincts.title: Data is a product with a contract.
instincts.framing: Datasets are products with consumers. Treat them like code: a declared schema, a compatibility policy, data lineage, tests, and an SLA. Verify, do not assume.

instincts.title: Enforce policy at the chokepoint.
instincts.framing: Security enforced in every application drifts and leaks. Enforce policy once at the layer everyone must pass, the catalog or engine, so it is consistent and auditable.

instincts.title: Cost is an architecture decision.
instincts.framing: The cloud bill is a function of design, not an afterthought. Bytes scanned, bytes moved across regions, storage tiering, and the compute purchasing model are all chosen at architecture time.

instincts.title: Meaning becomes geometry.
instincts.framing: Embeddings map meaning into vectors so that semantic similarity becomes distance. Retrieval becomes a geometry problem, and storage and indexing choices follow from that.

instincts.title: Feed the accelerator.
instincts.framing: A GPU starves unless the data pipeline keeps up; the bottleneck is bandwidth, not compute. Training-data systems exist to keep accelerators fed with correct, reproducible data.

nodes.name: Row vs Columnar
nodes.what: Row layout stores all fields of a record together; columnar layout stores each field contiguously. Point lookups favor rows; scans and aggregations favor columns.
nodes.analogy: A row store is a filing cabinet with one folder per customer. A column store is a spreadsheet where each attribute is its own strip you can sum without opening folders.

code.text:
-- columnar engine reads only the 2 columns it needs, not whole rows
SELECT asset_class, sum(market_value) FROM positions GROUP BY asset_class;

nodes.name: Encoding Schemes
nodes.what: Dictionary encoding, run-length encoding, bit-packing, and delta encoding shrink columnar data by exploiting repetition and ranges. They run before any general compressor, so the compressor then has less to do.
nodes.analogy: Instead of writing "GBP" a million times, write it once in a legend and store small ticket numbers that point at it.

code.text:
# trades.currency has few distinct values, so a dictionary stores small codes
# pyarrow applies dictionary encoding and bit-packing automatically on Parquet write

nodes.name: Compression Codecs
nodes.what: Snappy, LZ4, ZSTD, and gzip trade CPU cycles for fewer bytes moved. The right pick depends on whether the workload is I/O bound or CPU bound.
nodes.analogy: Vacuum-packing luggage. Tighter packing saves cargo space but costs time at both ends.

code.text:
# ZSTD for cold, rarely read data; Snappy for hot, frequently scanned data
df.write.option("compression", "zstd").parquet(path)

nodes.name: Apache Arrow
nodes.what: Apache Arrow is a language-agnostic in-memory columnar layout standard. A shared format lets engines and libraries pass data without re-serializing it.
nodes.analogy: A common shipping pallet size. Any forklift in any warehouse can move it without repacking.
ideas: the file and row group and page hierarchy

code.text:
import pyarrow as pa
t = pa.table({"trade_id": [1, 2], "price": [99.5, 100.0]})  # shareable, no recopy

nodes.name: Copies and SerDe
nodes.what: Every boundary at the operating system, process, and runtime level can force a buffer copy. Converting between formats, called SerDe, is often the largest single cost in a pipeline.
nodes.analogy: Each time a parcel changes courier it gets unpacked and repacked. The repacking, not the driving, eats the day.

code.text:
# avoid: df.toPandas() then rebuilding a Spark frame forces a full SerDe round trip
# prefer: stay in one engine, or hand off through Arrow

nodes.name: Zero-Copy
nodes.what: mmap, shared memory, and Arrow IPC let two consumers read the same bytes with no copy. The fastest copy is the one you never make.
nodes.analogy: Two people reading one whiteboard instead of each transcribing it into a notebook.

code.text:
import pyarrow as pa
buf = pa.memory_map("trades.arrow")  # read without copying into the heap

nodes.name: The Shuffle
nodes.what: A shuffle redistributes rows across the cluster by key so all rows with the same key meet. It writes to disk, transfers over the network, and reads back, which makes it the most expensive distributed operation.
nodes.analogy: Telling a stadium crowd to re-seat themselves by birth month. Everyone moves at once and the aisles jam.

code.text: df.groupBy("customer_id").sum("amount")  # triggers a shuffle on customer_id

nodes.name: Columnar Transport
nodes.what: Arrow Flight and Flight SQL move columnar batches over the wire without row-by-row encoding. This removes SerDe from the network path.
nodes.analogy: Shipping a pre-loaded container instead of handing over boxes one at a time at the dock.

code.text:
# a Flight SQL client receives Arrow batches directly, with no per-row parse
# client.execute("SELECT * FROM trades").read_all()

nodes.name: Volcano Model
nodes.what: The classic Volcano model iterator pulls one tuple at a time through a tree of operators. It is simple and composable, but the per-tuple function-call overhead is large.
nodes.analogy: Passing one brick at a time down a line of workers, each pausing to receive and hand off.

code.text: # conceptual: for row in child.next(): emit(predicate(row))  # one call per row

nodes.name: Vectorized Execution
nodes.what: Operators process batches of values, for example 1024 at a time, so the CPU runs tight loops and SIMD instructions. This amortizes dispatch cost across the whole batch.
nodes.analogy: Stamping a whole sheet of forms in one press rather than one form per pull of the lever.

code.text:
import polars as pl
pl.scan_parquet("trades.parquet").filter(pl.col("price") > 100).collect()  # batched

nodes.name: Code Generation
nodes.what: Whole-stage code generation and JIT compile a query fragment into specialized machine code. It removes interpreter overhead and is used by Photon, DuckDB, and Velox.
nodes.analogy: Casting a custom tool for one job instead of reaching for a general adjustable wrench on every turn.

code.text:
# Spark fuses filter, project, and aggregate into one generated function
# inspect with df.explain(mode="codegen")

nodes.name: Three Plan Levels
nodes.what: A query exists at three levels: the logical plan (what), the physical plan (how, with chosen operators), and the DAG of distributed tasks that actually runs. An expert can point to any one of the three on demand.
nodes.analogy: A trip is a destination, then a chosen route and transport, then the turn-by-turn legs you drive.

code.text:
EXPLAIN
SELECT c.country, sum(p.amount)
FROM payments p JOIN customers c USING(customer_id)
GROUP BY 1;

nodes.name: Lazy vs Eager
nodes.what: Lazy engines such as Spark, Polars, and Dask build the full plan before executing and optimize across the whole query. Eager engines such as pandas run each step at once and cannot reorder.
nodes.analogy: A chef reading the whole recipe before starting versus shopping for each ingredient mid-cook.

code.text:
import polars as pl
q = pl.scan_parquet("payments.parquet").filter(pl.col("amount") > 1e6)  # nothing runs
q.collect()  # plan is optimized, then executed

nodes.name: Rule-Based Optimization
nodes.what: Mechanical rewrites that are always safe: predicate pushdown, projection pushdown, constant folding. They shrink the work before any cost is considered.
nodes.analogy: Crossing items off a shopping list that you already have at home before driving to the store.

code.text: # WHERE amount > 1e6 is pushed into the Parquet scan; non-matching row groups are skipped

nodes.name: Cost-Based Optimization
nodes.what: Using table statistics such as row counts, number of distinct values, and histograms to choose join order, join algorithm, and physical operators by estimated cost. The optimizer is only as good as the statistics it is given.
nodes.analogy: A navigation app picking a route from live traffic estimates rather than always taking the straightest line.
ideas: optimization criteria such as latency versus shuffle bytes

code.text: ANALYZE TABLE trades COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR COLUMNS instrument_id;  -- feeds the cost model

nodes.name: Adaptive Query Execution
nodes.what: Adaptive query execution replans mid-flight using real runtime statistics. For example it switches a sort-merge join to a broadcast join once it sees the true build-side size.
nodes.analogy: Re-routing your drive after you actually hit the jam, not just from the morning forecast.

code.text: spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.enabled", "true")  # coalesce partitions, fix skew at runtime

nodes.name: Data Skipping
nodes.what: Min and max zone maps and bloom filters stored per file or block let the engine skip data that cannot match a predicate. This avoids I/O entirely rather than scanning faster.
nodes.analogy: A library catalog telling you a wing holds no books in your subject, so you never walk in.

code.text: -- row-group min and max on trade_ts let WHERE trade_ts >= DATE '2025-01-01' skip old groups

nodes.name: Pushdown
nodes.what: Pushing predicates, projections, aggregates, limits, and even joins down into the storage layer or remote source. Less data is read and transferred as a result.
nodes.analogy: Asking the warehouse to ship only the red size-10 shoes instead of shipping everything and sorting at home.

code.text: spark.read.parquet(path).select("trade_id", "price").filter("venue = 'LSE'")  # both pushed

nodes.name: Partitioning Strategies
nodes.what: Hash partitioning, range partitioning, and round-robin partitioning decide how rows map to partitions. The right choice aligns physical placement with the query's filter and join keys.
nodes.analogy: Filing invoices by month, which is range, versus by client initial, which is hash, depending on how you search them.

code.text: df.write.partitionBy("trade_date").parquet(path)  # filters on trade_date prune directories

nodes.name: Bucketing and Clustering
nodes.what: Persisting a partitioning or sort order to storage so future queries skip the shuffle or skip files. Clustering co-locates related rows physically.
nodes.analogy: Pre-sorting the mailroom shelves so the daily delivery never has to be re-sorted.

code.text: df.write.bucketBy(64, "customer_id").sortBy("customer_id").saveAsTable("payments_bucketed")

nodes.name: Sketches
nodes.what: Compact probabilistic summaries: HyperLogLog for distinct counts, t-digest for quantiles, Count-Min sketch for frequencies. They use constant memory, are mergeable, and have bounded error.
nodes.analogy: Estimating crowd size from one sampled section rather than counting every head.

code.text: SELECT approx_count_distinct(customer_id) FROM payments;  -- HyperLogLog under the hood

nodes.name: ANN Tradeoff
nodes.what: Approximate nearest neighbor search trades a little recall for large latency gains. Every vector index exposes a recall, latency, and build-cost triangle.
nodes.analogy: Asking a few well-connected locals for the nearest cafe instead of measuring the distance to every cafe in the city.

code.text:
# HNSW: a higher ef_search raises recall and latency together
# index.search(query_vec, k=10, ef_search=128)

nodes.name: Learned Indexes
nodes.what: Replacing a traditional index with a model that predicts the storage position of a key. It works when the key distribution is learnable.
nodes.analogy: Guessing a word's page in a dictionary from its first letter instead of binary searching every time.

code.text: # model maps key -> approximate offset, then a small local search corrects the guess

nodes.name: Narrow vs Wide Dependencies
nodes.what: Narrow dependencies such as map and filter need no data movement. Wide dependencies such as group-by and joins on unaligned keys require a shuffle, and that boundary defines stage boundaries.
nodes.analogy: Narrow is each worker finishing their own pile. Wide is everyone stopping to swap piles by category.

code.text:
df.filter("amount > 1e6")        # narrow, no shuffle
df.groupBy("account_id").count() # wide, shuffle

nodes.name: Distributed Joins
nodes.what: Broadcast join ships a small table to every node; shuffle hash join repartitions both sides by key; sort-merge join sorts both sides. Size and skew pick the winner.
nodes.analogy: Broadcast hands everyone a pocket reference. Shuffle re-seats both groups by key. Sort-merge lines both up in order and walks them together.

code.text:
from pyspark.sql.functions import broadcast
trades.join(broadcast(instruments), "instrument_id")  # ship the small dimension everywhere

nodes.name: Broadcast vs Shuffle
nodes.what: The deciding factors are the build-side size estimate, selectivity, and skew. A wrong estimate turns a cheap broadcast join into an out-of-memory failure or forces a needless shuffle.
nodes.analogy: Deciding whether to mail everyone a copy or call a central meeting depends on how big the document is.

code.text: spark.conf.set("spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold", 10 * 1024 * 1024)  # 10 MB cutoff

nodes.name: Skew and Salting
nodes.what: Skew is when a few keys hold most of the rows and overload one task. Salting splits hot keys across tasks, and adaptive query execution can detect and split skewed partitions.
nodes.analogy: One checkout lane jammed because everyone buys the same item, so you open extra lanes just for that item.

code.text:
from pyspark.sql.functions import concat_ws, rand
# spread a hot customer_id across 8 sub-keys, join, then aggregate the salt away
df.withColumn("k_salt", concat_ws("_", "customer_id", (rand() * 8).cast("int")))

nodes.name: Spilling
nodes.what: When an operator exceeds its memory budget it spills to disk and continues, trading speed for completion. Spill-aware operators degrade gracefully instead of failing.
nodes.analogy: Running out of desk space and stacking overflow papers on the floor rather than stopping work.

code.text:
# external sort writes sorted runs to disk then merges them
# spill bytes show up in the Spark UI stage metrics

nodes.name: Write-Ahead Log
nodes.what: Every change is appended to a write-ahead log and flushed before the data pages change. A crash is recovered by replaying the log, which makes the log the foundational durability primitive.
nodes.analogy: Writing your move in a notebook before you move the piece, so an interrupted game can resume exactly.

code.text:
# append the change record, fsync, then mutate pages; replay the log on restart
# a payment insert is durable once its log record is flushed

nodes.name: B-Tree
nodes.what: The workhorse OLTP index: balanced and sorted, strong for point lookups and range scans, updated in place. It is the default index behind most relational engines.
nodes.analogy: A library index with sorted tabs you binary-search instead of walking every shelf.

code.text: CREATE INDEX idx_accounts_id ON accounts(account_id);  -- B-tree for point lookups and ranges

nodes.name: LSM Tree
nodes.what: Writes buffer in an in-memory memtable, flush to sorted SSTables, and merge through compaction. This design dominates write-heavy systems because writes are sequential.
nodes.analogy: Jotting notes on sticky pads, then periodically filing them in sorted order.

code.text:
# writes land in a memtable, flush to immutable SSTables, compaction merges and sorts
# suits high-rate trade capture and surveillance event stores

nodes.name: MVCC
nodes.what: Multi-version concurrency control lets readers see a consistent snapshot while writers create new versions. Readers and writers do not block each other.
nodes.analogy: Each reader gets a photocopy of the ledger as of the moment they started reading.

code.text:
-- a long report reads a consistent snapshot while new trades keep arriving
SELECT count(*) FROM trades;  -- sees the data as of statement start

nodes.name: Copy-on-Write vs Merge-on-Read
nodes.what: Copy-on-write rewrites whole files on update, which is read-fast and write-heavy. Merge-on-read writes small deltas merged at read time, which is write-fast and read-heavier. The same tradeoff reappears in lakehouse formats.
nodes.analogy: Re-typing the whole document on every edit versus keeping a list of edits applied only when someone reads it.

code.text:
# copy-on-write rewrites the data file; merge-on-read appends a delta merged on read
# revised reinsurance claims favor merge-on-read for cheap frequent updates

nodes.name: Event Time vs Processing Time
nodes.what: Event time is when something happened, ingestion time is when it arrived, and processing time is when the engine handled it. Late and out-of-order data make the three diverge.
nodes.analogy: A postcard carries the date it was written, the postmark date, and the day you finally read it.
nodes.example_note: Reinsurance fits well: loss_date is event time, report_date is ingestion time

code.text:
-- claims can be reported years after the loss occurs
SELECT date_trunc('year', loss_date) AS yr, count(*) FROM claims GROUP BY 1;

nodes.name: Watermarks
nodes.what: A watermark is the engine's estimate that no events older than time T will still arrive, which lets it close a window. Completeness is therefore a bet, not a certainty.
nodes.analogy: Closing the ballot box at a deadline, accepting that a few late votes may be lost.

code.text: events.withWatermark("trade_ts", "10 minutes").groupBy(window("trade_ts", "1 minute")).count()

nodes.name: Windowing
nodes.what: Tumbling, sliding, and session windows cut an unbounded stream into finite chunks to aggregate. The window type encodes the business question.
nodes.analogy: Counting traffic per fixed hour, per rolling hour, or per gap-separated rush.

code.text:
from pyspark.sql.functions import window
payments.groupBy(window("payment_ts", "1 minute")).sum("amount")  # tumbling 1-minute windows

nodes.name: Ordering Guarantees
nodes.what: Ordering holds within a partition but not across partitions, and global order costs coordination. The partition key decides what stays ordered.
nodes.analogy: Each checkout line is ordered, but there is no single order across all the lines.
ideas: wall-clock versus logical clocks versus hybrid logical clocks

code.text:
# Kafka preserves order within a partition keyed by account_id, not across partitions
# choose the partition key so per-account events stay ordered

nodes.name: Idempotency and Determinism
nodes.what: Idempotent writes produce the same end state on re-run; deterministic transforms produce the same output for the same input. Together with replayability they make recovery safe.
nodes.analogy: A switch set to on lands in the same state no matter how many times you push it toward on.

nodes.name: Change Data Capture
nodes.what: Change data capture turns a source database's changes into a stream by reading its log, using triggers, or polling queries. Log-based CDC has the lowest impact on the source.
nodes.analogy: Subscribing to a newspaper's corrections column instead of re-reading the whole paper each day.

code.text:
# log-based CDC reads the database write-ahead log and emits row changes
# Debezium streams accounts inserts, updates, and deletes into the lakehouse

nodes.name: Incremental View Maintenance
nodes.what: Updating a materialized view from only the changed rows instead of recomputing from scratch. Cost scales with the change size, not the table size.
nodes.analogy: Updating a running total by adding the new receipt, not re-adding every receipt.

code.text:
# maintain daily position totals from only the new trades
# new_total = prev_total + sum(changed_rows)

nodes.name: Slowly Changing Dimensions
nodes.what: Slowly changing dimensions track attribute changes over time: Type 1 overwrites, Type 2 adds a versioned row, Type 3 keeps a prior-value column. The type chosen decides how much history survives.
nodes.analogy: Type 1 erases the old address, Type 2 keeps a dated address history, Type 3 keeps only the previous address.

code.text:
-- SCD Type 2: close the old risk_rating row and insert a new versioned row
-- keeps full history of customers.risk_rating with valid_from and valid_to

nodes.name: Bitemporal Modeling
nodes.what: Bitemporal modeling tracks valid time, when a fact was true in the world, and system time, when the system recorded it. This enables true audit and as-of reconstruction.
nodes.analogy: A ledger that records both when an event occurred and when you wrote it down, so you can ask what you believed last Tuesday.
nodes.example_note: Reinsurance fits strongly: claim reserve revisions over time

nodes.name: Object Storage Semantics
nodes.what: Object stores such as S3, GCS, and ADLS meter listing, lack atomic rename, and were historically eventually consistent. Files are immutable, so you replace rather than edit.
nodes.analogy: A mail depot where you can drop and fetch parcels but never reach in to edit one.

code.text:
# no in-place edit and no atomic rename; write a new object and swap a pointer
# listing a prefix with millions of objects is slow and metered

nodes.name: Lakehouse Premise
nodes.what: A metadata layer brings warehouse semantics such as ACID, schema, and time travel to object-storage economics. It does so by separating the metadata layer from the file layer.
nodes.analogy: A card catalog that turns a warehouse of unmarked boxes into a queryable library.

code.text: # a metadata layer over immutable Parquet files provides ACID, schema, and time travel

nodes.name: Iceberg Internals
nodes.what: Iceberg defines a table with a metadata tree of snapshots and manifests, supports hidden partitioning and safe schema evolution. v2 and v3 add row-level and equality deletes and deletion vectors.
nodes.analogy: A versioned table of contents that lists exactly which files make up the table right now.

code.text:
-- read the table as of an older snapshot
SELECT * FROM trades FOR SYSTEM_VERSION AS OF 8273465;

nodes.name: Delta Internals
nodes.what: Delta Lake uses a transaction log of JSON commits plus Parquet checkpoints, and deletion vectors for merge-on-read deletes. UniForm exposes Iceberg-compatible metadata for the same files.
nodes.analogy: A commit log like version control, periodically snapshotted so you never replay from the beginning.

code.text:
# _delta_log holds JSON commits plus periodic Parquet checkpoints
# deletion vectors mark deleted rows without rewriting the data file

nodes.name: Hudi and Format Choice
nodes.what: Hudi offers copy-on-write and merge-on-read tables with a timeline of instants and record-level indexes. Choosing among Iceberg, Delta Lake, Hudi, and Lance is a function of write pattern, ecosystem, and AI needs.
nodes.analogy: Picking a filing system by how often you revise documents versus how often you read them.

code.text:
# copy-on-write for read-heavy tables, merge-on-read for write-heavy tables
# pick Iceberg, Delta, Hudi, or Lance by write pattern, ecosystem, and AI needs

nodes.name: Table Maintenance
nodes.what: Compaction merges small files, vacuum and snapshot expiration remove dead files, and Z-order clustering improves skipping. Unmaintained lakehouse tables degrade in both cost and speed.
nodes.analogy: Defragmenting the cabinet and shredding old drafts so it stays fast and lean.

code.text:
OPTIMIZE trades ZORDER BY (trade_ts);  -- compact small files and cluster for skipping
VACUUM trades RETAIN 168 HOURS;        -- expire dead files

nodes.name: Disaggregated Storage and Compute
nodes.what: Separating storage from compute lets each scale on its own and lets compute be ephemeral. It is the defining pattern of every modern cloud data system.
nodes.analogy: Renting meeting rooms by the hour while your files live permanently in a vault.

code.text: # data lives in object storage; compute clusters spin up, read, and shut down

nodes.name: Pluggable Backends
nodes.what: Velox, DataFusion, Photon, and Gluten are drop-in vectorized execution layers reused across engines. They let several products share one high-performance core.
nodes.analogy: A standard engine block that several car brands bolt their own body onto.

code.text: # Velox, DataFusion, Photon, and Gluten provide a reusable vectorized execution layer

nodes.name: Engine Architectures
nodes.what: MPP engines like Trino and Redshift, embedded engines like DuckDB and Polars, and scale-out engines like Spark and ClickHouse suit different sizes and latencies. The right class depends on data size and concurrency, not fashion.
nodes.analogy: A fleet, a hatchback, and a freight train for different journeys.

code.text: # MPP: Trino, Redshift  |  embedded: DuckDB, Polars  |  scale-out: Spark, ClickHouse

nodes.name: Substrait
nodes.what: Substrait is a cross-engine logical plan intermediate representation. One frontend can target many backends, and one backend can serve many frontends.
nodes.analogy: A shared blueprint format that any builder can read and construct from.

code.text: # one logical plan IR; a Polars or Spark frontend can target many execution backends

nodes.name: Federation
nodes.what: Federation runs one query across heterogeneous engines and sources, pushing work to each. It avoids copying data just to query it.
nodes.analogy: A general contractor coordinating specialist trades, each doing its part on site.

code.text:
-- one query joins a Postgres table and a Parquet lake table, pushing filters to each
SELECT * FROM postgres.customers c JOIN lake.payments p USING(customer_id);

nodes.name: Memory Budgets
nodes.what: Per-operator memory accounting decides whether a query spills or fails. On-heap versus off-heap placement changes garbage-collection behavior under load.
nodes.analogy: Each department gets a budget; overspending triggers a spill to a cheaper option, not bankruptcy.

code.text: spark.conf.set("spark.memory.fraction", "0.6")  # operator budget; overflow spills, not crashes

nodes.name: Backpressure and Admission Control
nodes.what: Backpressure slows producers when consumers fall behind; admission control queues or rejects new work to protect SLAs. Both bound the work in flight.
nodes.analogy: A busy restaurant pacing the kitchen and pausing the waitlist rather than seating everyone at once.

code.text: # slow producers when the sink lags; queue or reject new queries to protect SLAs

nodes.name: Schedulers and Autoscaling
nodes.what: YARN and Kubernetes allocate resources; autoscaling adds or removes capacity reactively or predictively. The policy trades dollar cost against latency.
nodes.analogy: Calling in more staff when the queue grows and sending them home when it shrinks.

code.text: # Kubernetes allocates executors; dynamic allocation adds and removes them with load

nodes.name: Multi-Tenancy and Isolation
nodes.what: Resource queues, namespaces, and concurrency limits stop one tenant's heavy job from starving others. This is the cure for the noisy neighbor problem.
nodes.analogy: Separate lanes on a shared road so one truck does not block every car.

code.text: # separate resource queues per team stop one heavy job from starving the others

nodes.name: Data Lineage
nodes.what: Dataset-level and column-level data lineage records where data came from and what it feeds. It enables impact analysis and faster debugging.
nodes.analogy: A supply-chain label tracing every ingredient back to its farm.

code.text:
# emit OpenLineage events so each dataset records its inputs and consumers
# column-level lineage shows report.total derives from payments.amount

nodes.name: Schema Evolution
nodes.what: Forward, backward, and full compatibility rules govern safe schema change. The wrong change silently breaks consumers downstream.
nodes.analogy: Updating a form so that both old and new printed copies remain readable.

code.text: ALTER TABLE customers ADD COLUMN segment STRING;  -- a nullable add is backward compatible

nodes.name: Data Contracts
nodes.what: A data contract is a producer-consumer agreement with an explicit schema, semantics, and SLA. It is enforced in CI so a breaking change fails the build.
nodes.analogy: A delivery contract specifying size, timing, and quality, signed by both sides.

code.text: # the producer of payments commits to a schema, freshness SLA, and semantics; CI checks it

nodes.name: Data Quality
nodes.what: Frameworks like Great Expectations, Soda, and dbt tests assert row counts, ranges, and uniqueness. They act as a gate that stops bad data from propagating.
nodes.analogy: Quality-control sampling on a production line that halts the belt on a defect.

code.text:
# Great Expectations: assert amount is non-negative and customer_id is present
expect_column_values_to_be_between("amount", min_value=0, max_value=None)

nodes.name: Testing for Data
nodes.what: Unit tests on pure transforms, property-based testing, snapshot testing, and data diffing catch regressions before release. Pipelines become testable when transforms are pure functions.
nodes.analogy: A test suite plus a before-and-after photo comparison for every change.

code.text:
# unit test a pure transform on a tiny banking fixture
assert normalize_currency("gbp") == "GBP"

nodes.name: Catalog as Policy
nodes.what: The catalog above the engines is the natural place to enforce access, since every engine consults it. Policy lives in one place rather than in each application.
nodes.analogy: One staffed gate to the building instead of a separate lock on every interior door.

code.text: GRANT SELECT ON payments TO ROLE analyst_emea;  -- enforced at the catalog, every engine obeys

nodes.name: Row and Column Security
nodes.what: Row-level security and column-level security restrict which rows and columns a principal can see. Enforcement sits at the catalog or engine layer.
nodes.analogy: A redacted document where your clearance decides which lines and pages appear.
nodes.example_note: Banking: analysts see only their region's accounts

code.text:
-- analysts see only rows for their own branches
CREATE ROW FILTER region_filter ON accounts AS (branch_id IN current_user_branches());

nodes.name: Masking and Tokenization
nodes.what: Masking irreversibly obscures a value; tokenization swaps it for a reversible token held in a vault. Both protect sensitive banking fields such as PII and card numbers.
nodes.analogy: Blacking out a number versus replacing it with a claim-check stub you can later redeem.

code.text: # masking blanks the card number to XXXX; tokenization swaps it for a reversible vault token

nodes.name: Encryption
nodes.what: Encryption at rest and in transit with envelope encryption and bring-your-own-key, plus key rotation. It protects data even when storage or network is compromised.
nodes.analogy: A locked container plus a master key you control and can change at will.

code.text: # envelope encryption: a data key encrypts data, a master key (BYOK) encrypts the data key

nodes.name: Cost Units
nodes.what: Reason in dollars per TB scanned, per query, and per GB stored as first-class design constraints. These units, not finance trivia, drive layout and pruning choices.
nodes.analogy: Pricing a journey by fuel per mile before choosing the route.

code.text:
-- a serverless engine bills by bytes scanned; pruning columns and partitions cuts the bill
SELECT trade_id, price FROM trades WHERE trade_date = DATE '2025-06-01';

nodes.name: Storage Tiering
nodes.what: Hot, warm, cold, and archive tiers trade retrieval speed for storage price. Lifecycle policies move data between tiers automatically.
nodes.analogy: Desk drawer, filing cabinet, basement, and off-site vault for how often you reach for it.
nodes.example_note: Reinsurance: old claims age into archive storage

code.text: # lifecycle policy moves claims older than 2 years from hot to archive storage

nodes.name: Compute Purchasing
nodes.what: Spot, on-demand, and reserved capacity trade price against reliability and commitment. The mix should match how interruptible each workload is.
nodes.analogy: Standby tickets, walk-up fares, and a season pass for different travel needs.

code.text: # spot for fault-tolerant batch, reserved for steady baseline, on-demand for spikes

nodes.name: Cache vs Recompute
nodes.what: Caching trades storage and staleness risk for repeated-query speed. Sometimes recomputing is cheaper than maintaining a cache.
nodes.analogy: Keeping leftovers versus cooking fresh, depending on how often you eat the dish.

code.text: # cache a daily aggregate if it is queried often; recompute if rarely read or cheap to derive

nodes.name: Embeddings as Data
nodes.what: An embedding is a learned dense vector that represents an item's meaning. It is a first-class data type whose access pattern is similarity, not equality.
nodes.analogy: Placing every document on a map so that related ones sit near each other.

code.text:
# an embedding is a dense vector; store it next to the row it represents
vec = embed("counterparty due diligence note")  # shape (1024,)

nodes.name: Vector Indexes
nodes.what: HNSW is a navigable graph, IVF and IVF-PQ partition then search, and DiskANN and ScaNN serve billion-scale corpora from disk. Each exposes the recall, latency, and build-cost triangle.
nodes.analogy: Neighborhood maps and shortcuts so you find nearby points without checking the whole city.

code.text: # HNSW: graph of nearest neighbors  |  IVF and IVF-PQ: cluster then search  |  DiskANN: on disk

nodes.name: Vector Storage
nodes.what: Dedicated stores such as Pinecone, Weaviate, Milvus, and Qdrant, extensions such as pgvector and Elasticsearch, and Lance and LanceDB keep vectors near the data. The choice trades operational simplicity against unification with analytical columns.
nodes.analogy: A specialty library wing versus adding a vector shelf to the existing library.

code.text:
# Lance stores vectors and columns together; query by similarity
tbl.search(query_vec).limit(10).to_arrow()

nodes.name: Retrieval Modes
nodes.what: Sparse retrieval with BM25 over an inverted index matches terms; dense retrieval matches embeddings; hybrid search blends both with a reranker. The mix is chosen for recall and precision needs.
nodes.analogy: Keyword search, meaning search, and a judge who blends the two shortlists.

code.text: # sparse: BM25 over an inverted index  |  dense: embedding similarity  |  hybrid: blend + rerank

nodes.name: RAG as a Workload
nodes.what: RAG is a data pipeline: parse and chunk documents, index them, filter by metadata, keep fresh, and evaluate. It is data engineering, not a model trick.
nodes.analogy: Running a research desk that files sources, retrieves the right ones on demand, and audits its hit rate.
related: evaluation such as recall at k and MRR

code.text: # parse -> chunk -> embed -> index -> retrieve with metadata filter -> evaluate recall at k

nodes.name: Feature Stores
nodes.what: A feature store serves the same feature definitions online at low latency and offline in batch. This avoids online-offline skew between training and serving.
nodes.analogy: One recipe used in both the test kitchen and the restaurant so the dish never differs.
nodes.example_note: Banking: fraud features computed identically at scoring and training time

code.text: # define a fraud feature once; serve it online for scoring and offline for training

nodes.name: Point-in-Time Correctness
nodes.what: Assemble training data using only information available as of the label time. This avoids label leakage that inflates offline metrics.
nodes.analogy: Grading a forecast using only what was known before the event, never after.

code.text:
-- as-of join: attach only feature values known before the label timestamp
SELECT * FROM labels l ASOF JOIN features f ON f.entity = l.entity AND f.ts <= l.label_ts;

nodes.name: Data Loader as a System
nodes.what: The data loader is a CPU-to-GPU pipeline with workers, prefetch queues, and shared memory. It must hide I/O behind compute or the accelerator stalls.
nodes.analogy: A pit crew handing over the next tire before the car stops, so the engine never waits.

code.text:
# overlap I/O with compute so the GPU never waits
DataLoader(ds, num_workers=8, prefetch_factor=4, pin_memory=True)

nodes.name: Sharding and Streaming Datasets
nodes.what: File-level sharding and streaming formats such as WebDataset, MosaicML Streaming, and Lance stream training data from object storage. They avoid hot spots across many GPUs.
nodes.analogy: Dealing cards evenly from several decks so no single dealer is overworked.

code.text: # shard files evenly and stream from object storage with WebDataset, Mosaic, or Lance

nodes.name: GPU-Aware Formats
nodes.what: Random access on Parquet stalls training, so Lance and tfrecord shards exist for sequential and shardable reads. GPUDirect Storage loads data directly into device memory.
nodes.analogy: A conveyor that drops parts straight onto the assembly arm instead of routing through a stockroom.

code.text: # random reads on Parquet stall training; Lance and tfrecord shards plus GPUDirect load fast
